title: The Courts Have Condemned the AIADMK Government Countless Times!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The source scans restore an omitted clause in the Island Grounds case: senior advocate P. Wilson argued that officials acted in the AIADMK's favour, violating both the election code and the High Court's order. They also confirm the closing date as 17.4.2016. The Tamil reading copy below restores those source readings and repairs only other unambiguous OCR defects. The canonical Tamil source remains unchanged.
paragraphs: Under the DMK Government, the Anna Centenary Library—one of Asia's largest libraries—was built and opened by me. For that very reason, Jayalalithaa's AIADMK Government failed to carry out proper maintenance. Its attempts to turn the building into a children's hospital and a wedding hall were halted by court condemnation and fierce opposition from Tamil scholars and students.

Manonmani filed a case in the Madras High Court alleging that the AIADMK Government continued to neglect the library by failing to purchase necessary books and maintain its infrastructure. The First Bench comprising Chief Justice S.K. Kaul and Justice Pushpa Sathyanarayana heard the case.

Following an order issued by the Bench headed by the Chief Justice on 29 October 2015, court-appointed commissioners inspected whether the court's directions had been fully implemented and whether the library was being maintained. Their report, filed in the High Court on 8 January 2016, stated that none of the basic infrastructure had been improved.

The Anna Library case again came before the Chief Justice's Bench on 15 April 2016. Senior advocate P. Wilson argued: “Despite repeated orders from the High Court, no basic facilities have yet been provided at the Anna Library.”
The judges responded:

> “We strongly condemn the Tamil Nadu Government's conduct. Why has the Government not implemented the court's order? If the Government wishes, it can complete anything within forty-eight hours. Otherwise, it does nothing. Your work causes us anguish. We are setting a final deadline of 30 June. If the facilities are not provided by then, the court itself will take the Anna Library under its control, provide the basic facilities and begin maintaining it.”
The High Court thus issued an extremely stern warning to the Tamil Nadu Government.

This was not the first time the Madras High Court had condemned Jayalalithaa's Government over the Anna Library. Although the court repeatedly issued condemnation, strong condemnation and warnings, the AIADMK Government neither showed the slightest concern nor respected the High Court's orders and took appropriate action. Nor is this confined to the Anna Library case. Arrows of condemnation have continued to rain upon the AIADMK Government in many other cases. I shall explain just a few examples here.

This was not the first time the Madras High Court had condemned Jayalalithaa's Government over the Anna Library. Although the court repeatedly issued condemnation, strong condemnation and warnings, the AIADMK Government neither showed the slightest concern nor respected the High Court's orders and took appropriate action.

A case was filed in the Madras High Court concerning problems in the methods used to prepare Chennai's Island Grounds for Jayalalithaa's election campaign meeting. Senior advocate P. Wilson argued that officials had acted in favour of the AIADMK, that the election code of conduct had been violated, and that the High Court's order had also been breached. Accepting the argument and taking a serious view, the Madras High Court appointed a legal commissioner and ordered a committee under the commissioner's leadership to visit Island Grounds, inspect the conditions there and submit a report to the court. It is noteworthy that even the Election Commission did not properly comply with the High Court's 7 April 2016 order that “the existing position must continue.”

The DMK Government introduced Samacheer Kalvi, the Uniform System of School Education, to eliminate the disparity between rural students with fewer facilities and urban students with greater advantages. The AIADMK had barely returned to office in 2011 when it laid its hands on the scheme. It was, after all, a progressive programme introduced by the Kazhagam Government, an effort to eliminate inequality, deliver equal justice and create parity. Would the AIADMK rulers leave it alone?
The vile attempt to kill Samacheer Kalvi was taken to the Madras High Court. On 19 July 2011, the High Court sternly condemned the Jayalalithaa Government:

> “The Supreme Court ordered an examination of the standard of Samacheer Kalvi and the method of implementation. It did not order an inquiry into whether the scheme should be implemented this year or next. Without even understanding that, the Tamil Nadu Government submits a report saying Samacheer Kalvi cannot be implemented this year. The High Court had already ruled that the Samacheer Kalvi Act was valid, and the Supreme Court dismissed petitions challenging it. In these circumstances, the Tamil Nadu Government has suspended Samacheer Kalvi in the name of a legislative amendment. This is contrary to the Constitution, which protects the right to education.”

In a case concerning requirements under Medical Council of India rules—including fire-fighting and fire-prevention equipment and ramps in medical colleges and hospitals—the Madras High Court ordered the Tamil Nadu Government to file a report. The Government filed no counter-affidavit. When it sought additional time, the judges refused and condemned the conduct, asking: “How many times can an extension be granted? It is not right to request more time in this manner on every occasion.” The court also imposed a fine of ten thousand rupees upon the Tamil Nadu Government's Health Secretary.

After reports said that the juvenile home in Madurai lacked basic facilities, the Madras High Court registered a case on its own motion. An officer of the Tamil Nadu Social Welfare Department filed a report stating, among other things, that “a juvenile home with all facilities is to be built in Tiruchi.” After reading it, the judges said: “We asked for details about the Madurai juvenile home, but the Social Welfare Department officer has submitted details of a home to be built in Tiruchi. The officer has not even correctly understood the court's order.” Is the Jayalalithaa Government's response not like the proverb: “When asked for directions to Pattukkottai, he replies with the price of areca nut”?

On 28 June 2014, an eleven-storey building collapsed at Moulivakkam near Chennai, killing sixty-one workers. Kazhagam Treasurer M.K. Stalin filed a public-interest petition in the Madras High Court seeking a CBI investigation. He also asked the court to order publication of the report of the inquiry commission headed by Justice R. Regupathi into the collapse. The judges hearing the case warned the Jayalalithaa Government: “If there is delay in tabling the Regupathi Commission report in the Legislative Assembly, we may have to order a CBI investigation.”

When a court found Jayalalithaa guilty in the Kodaikanal Pleasant Stay Hotel case, a bus was set ablaze in Dharmapuri, and three women students of the Coimbatore Agricultural University were burned to death. T.K. Rajendran, who received a two-year prison sentence in that case, was elected Chairman of the Dharmapuri District Milk Producers' Cooperative Union immediately after leaving prison. A case was filed in the Madras High Court seeking to have that election declared invalid. The court condemned the matter, asking: “How was a person sentenced to imprisonment permitted to serve as chairman of a cooperative society?”

Chokkalingam filed a case in the Madras High Court seeking an order granting persons with disabilities three per cent reservation in promotions within Tamil Nadu Government departments. Although notices directed the Secretaries of the School Education and Higher Education Departments to file counter-affidavits, they failed to do so. Angered by this negligence, the Madras High Court strongly condemned the Tamil Nadu Government and fined each department ten thousand rupees. When the Higher Education Department failed to file a counter-affidavit even on the third occasion, the angry judges again imposed a fine of ten thousand rupees upon it.

Court condemnations, warnings, rebukes, fines and orders requiring personal appearance are events that should make a government shrink in shame and bow its head. The fact that Jayalalithaa's Government alone in India has encountered such shameful turns in so many cases brings no honour to Tamil Nadu; it inflicts humiliation upon humiliation.

And there are countless more cases like these! Court condemnations, warnings, rebukes, fines and orders requiring personal appearance are events that should make a government shrink in shame and bow its head. The fact that Jayalalithaa's Government alone in India has encountered such shameful turns in so many cases brings no honour to Tamil Nadu; it inflicts humiliation upon humiliation.
Yet the AIADMK Government is not troubled in the slightest. Treating every judicial condemnation as an arrow fired at a massive wall, Jayalalithaa's Government stands aside and watches the spectacle.
With affection,
M.K.
